articles/analysis.html.title: SDK Scope | viDoc SDK for Unity
articles/analysis.html.keywords: SDK Scope Package Scope The SDK is distributed as a Unity package located at Packages/com.vigram.sdk-gh1. It is intended to be added to an existing Unity mobile application, not opened as a standalone Unity sample project. SDK version 1.0.0 targets mobile Unity applications on iOS and Android. It wraps Bluetooth LE transport, viDoc peripheral protocol handling, GNSS/NMEA models, NTRIP correction streams, laser measurements, device motion, file logging, and SDK token authentication. Confirmed Package Targets Minimum Unity version: 2021.3. Package name: com.vigram.sdk-gh1. Target platforms: iOS, Android. Runtime assemblies: ViGram and ServicesAssembly. ViGram.asmdef references the precompiled VigramAuthenticityVerificator.dll. The Android plugin includes BLE permissions and BluetoothLE.androidlib. The iOS post-build processor adds NSBluetoothAlwaysUsageDescription. The public 1.0.0 API uses PascalCase members and interface names with the I prefix. NTRIP in 1.0.0 has explicit success/failure callbacks for connection startup and an error channel for correction data. IGPSService is created with both IPeripheralService and IBluetoothService. Main API Surface Application integrations normally work with these public entry points: Vigram factories. IAuthenticationService and AuthenticationResult. IBluetoothService, BPeripheral, and CBManagerState. IPeripheralService callbacks and high-level request methods. NmeaMessage, DeviceMessage, SatelliteMessage, and related public value types. INtripService and INtripTaskService. IGPSService. ILaserService and LaserConfiguration. IDeviceService. ILogger. Integration Assumptions SDK tokens are issued by an external viGram process. The package source does not include a public token issuance flow. Production apps should depend on an immutable version tag such as 1.0.0. Device selection UI is application-specific. SDK examples should show how to receive BPeripheral values, not prescribe a UI framework. Some runtime behavior, such as platform permission prompts, depends on Unity project settings and target OS versions. Content Architecture Section Purpose Primary reader Overview Understand what the SDK does New developer Installation And First Run Install the package and run the first scan Integrator SDK Architecture Understand package layers and lifecycle Integrator Core Services Find the correct public service and callback App developer Practical Recipes Copy integration patterns for common tasks Integrator Platforms And Build Validate iOS/Android requirements Mobile developer Migration To 1.0.0 Move from older naming and signatures Existing integration owner Troubleshooting Diagnose Bluetooth, peripheral, NTRIP, GPS, and laser failures QA / developer API Reference Inspect exact generated public types All developers

articles/architecture.html.title: SDK Architecture | viDoc SDK for Unity
articles/architecture.html.keywords: SDK Architecture Package Layers flowchart TD App["Unity application"] --> Vigram["Vigram factories"] Vigram --> Auth["IAuthenticationService"] Vigram --> Bluetooth["IBluetoothService"] Vigram --> Peripheral["IPeripheralService"] Vigram --> Ntrip["INtripService / INtripTaskService"] Vigram --> GPS["IGPSService"] Vigram --> Laser["ILaserService"] Vigram --> Motion["IDeviceService"] Vigram --> Logger["ILogger"] Bluetooth --> Services["Runtime/Services"] Services --> Native["Android/iOS native plugins"] Peripheral --> Models["Messages and models"] GPS --> Ntrip GPS --> Peripheral GPS --> Bluetooth Laser --> Peripheral Service Lifetime Vigram is a static factory surface, not a singleton container. Create service instances at the start of the owning session or screen and pass those instances to other components. The detailed ownership table is in Core Services. Directories Path Purpose Packages/com.vigram.sdk-gh1/package.json Unity Package Manager metadata Runtime/ViGram Main C# runtime API Runtime/Services Low-level Bluetooth LE and MotionManager bridges Runtime/Plugins/Android Android BLE library, manifest, and Gradle metadata Runtime/Plugins/iOS iOS native Bluetooth LE and MotionManager plugins Runtime/AuthenticityVerificator Precompiled authentication verification dependency Editor/PostBuildProcess.cs iOS post-build Info.plist update scripts/release Release scripts and binary/package stubs Assembly Definitions ViGram.asmdef: assembly name: ViGram; autoReferenced: true; overrideReferences: true; precompiled reference: VigramAuthenticityVerificator.dll; reference to ServicesAssembly. ServicesAssembly.asmdef: assembly name: ServicesAssembly; contains low-level Unity/native bridge code; autoReferenced: true. Runtime Lifecycle Authenticate the SDK with Vigram.Initial(token).Check(...). Create one IBluetoothService for the viDoc session. Subscribe to State and ObserveAvailableDevices. Start scanning with StartScan(). Let the application choose a BPeripheral. Call IBluetoothService.Connect(peripheral) on the same Bluetooth service instance. Create an IPeripheralService. Subscribe to state, NMEA, device, satellite, and diagnostic callbacks. Call IPeripheralService.Start(). Create and store INtripService, INtripTaskService, IGPSService, and ILaserService only when the application needs correction streams, GPS aggregation, or laser measurements. Stop scans, disconnect Bluetooth, stop NTRIP tasks, and stop motion updates on the same service instances when the owning Unity object is destroyed or the user ends the session. Scene And Inspector Model The package does not provide Unity scenes, prefabs, or ScriptableObject assets. Services are created through Vigram factories from C# code. Documentation therefore focuses on runtime integration patterns rather than Inspector fields.

articles/getting-started.html.title: Installation And First Run | viDoc SDK for Unity
articles/getting-started.html.keywords: Installation And First Run Install Through Package Manager In Unity, open Window > Package Manager, press +, select Add package from git URL..., and enter: https://github.com/vigram-sw/sdk-unity-vidoc-distribution.git?path=/Packages/com.vigram.sdk-gh1#1.0.0 You can also add the dependency directly to Packages/manifest.json: { "dependencies": { "com.vigram.sdk-gh1": "https://github.com/vigram-sw/sdk-unity-vidoc-distribution.git?path=/Packages/com.vigram.sdk-gh1#1.0.0" } } For production projects, prefer an immutable tag such as #1.0.0 over a branch name. Use A Local Checkout For SDK development or local validation, use Add package from disk... and select: Packages/com.vigram.sdk-gh1/package.json Service Lifetime Rule Vigram service members are factories. They do not return shared singleton instances for the active app session. Create a service once in the owning session or screen and pass the same instance around. This matters most for Bluetooth: create one IBluetoothService, scan with it, stop scanning with it, connect with it, pass it into Vigram.GPSService(...), and disconnect with it. First Run Flow This example shows the complete Unity lifecycle for the first viDoc connection. It uses MonoBehaviour only to demonstrate Start() and OnDestroy(); the SDK services themselves are ordinary C# objects. using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class VidocFirstRunExample : MonoBehaviour { private IBluetoothService bluetooth; private IPeripheralService peripheral; private bool isConnecting; private void Start() { Vigram.Initial("YOUR_SDK_TOKEN").Check(OnAuthenticated); } private void OnAuthenticated(AuthenticationResult result) { if (result is AuthenticationResult.Error error) { Debug.LogError($"viDoc SDK authorization failed: {error.Enum} {error.Message}"); return; } bluetooth = Vigram.BluetoothService; bluetooth.State(state => Debug.Log($"Bluetooth state: {state}")); bluetooth.ObserveAvailableDevices(OnDevicesChanged); bluetooth.StartScan(); } private void OnDevicesChanged(List<BPeripheral> devices) { if (isConnecting || devices.Count == 0) { return; } // Replace this with your device picker UI. Connect(devices[0]); } private void Connect(BPeripheral selectedPeripheral) { isConnecting = true; bluetooth.StopScan(); bluetooth.Connect(selectedPeripheral); peripheral = Vigram.PeripheralService(selectedPeripheral); peripheral.State = state => Debug.Log($"Peripheral state: {state}"); peripheral.ConfigurationState = state => Debug.Log($"Configuration: {state.GetType().Name}"); peripheral.Nmea = OnNmeaMessage; peripheral.DeviceMessages = message => Debug.Log($"Device message: {message.GetType().Name}"); peripheral.SatelliteMessages = message => Debug.Log($"Satellite message: {message.GetType().Name}"); peripheral.Start(); } private void OnNmeaMessage(NmeaMessage message) { switch (message) { case NmeaMessage.Gga gga: Debug.Log($"GGA coordinate: {gga.Coordinate?.Latitude}, {gga.Coordinate?.Longitude}"); break; case NmeaMessage.Gst gst: Debug.Log($"GST accuracy: H={gst.Accuracy.Horizontal} V={gst.Accuracy.Vertical}"); break; } } private void OnDestroy() { bluetooth?.StopScan(); bluetooth?.Disconnect(); } } The SDK does not provide a device picker UI. ObserveAvailableDevices can fire multiple times while scanning, so production apps should show the latest list and let the user choose the intended BPeripheral. Initial Peripheral Configuration Pass PeripheralConfiguration to Vigram.PeripheralService(...) when the app needs a known startup profile for GNSS rate, elevation mask, constellation state, NAV-DOP/NAV-PVT messages, or dynamic model. PeripheralConfiguration configuration = new( rateOfChangeMessages: RateValue.Rate.Hertz7, elevationType: ElevationValue.Elevation.Angle10, glonassActivate: true, beidouActivate: true, galileoActivate: true, qzssActivate: true, sbassActivate: true, navDOPActivate: true, navPVTActivate: true, dynamicType: DynamicStateType.Pedestrian); IPeripheralService peripheral = Vigram.PeripheralService( selectedPeripheral, configuration: configuration); Subscribe to ConfigurationState before calling Start() so the application can show progress or failure details. File Logging Create the logger before creating the logged peripheral service. using System.IO; using UnityEngine; using ViGram; string logPath = Path.Combine(Application.persistentDataPath, "vidoc-session.csv"); ILogger logger = Vigram.LoggerService(logPath); IPeripheralService peripheral = Vigram.PeripheralService(selectedPeripheral, logger: logger); Use logging during integration, field tests, and support sessions. For detailed logging guidance, see Log A Full Session.

articles/migration.html.title: Migration To 1.0.0 | viDoc SDK for Unity
articles/migration.html.keywords: Migration To 1.0.0 Version 1.0.0 is a major update. According to the package changelog and current source, it adds Android support, HTTPS NTRIP options, explicit NTRIP callbacks, structured GPS disconnect notifications, and binary distribution changes. Main Breaking Changes Before 1.0.0 In 1.0.0 Interfaces without the I prefix IBluetoothService, IPeripheralService, IGPSService, INtripService lowerCamelCase methods and properties PascalCase API Vigram.Init(token) Vigram.Initial(token) Vigram.bluetoothService Vigram.BluetoothService bluetooth.startScan() bluetooth.StartScan() bluetooth.connect(peripheral) bluetooth.Connect(peripheral) Vigram.peripheralService(peripheral) Vigram.PeripheralService(peripheral) peripheral.start() peripheral.Start() Method-style NMEA callbacks Assign IPeripheralService.Nmea Vigram.gpsService(peripheral, ntripTask) Vigram.GPSService(peripheral, bluetoothService, ntripTask, automaticallyReconnectNtrip) NTRIP Start() without handshake result Start(actionSuccess, actionFailure) NTRIP data callback without an error channel Data(Action<byte[], Exception>) Separate GGAMessage, GSTMessage, TXTMessage types NmeaMessage.Gga, NmeaMessage.Gst, NmeaMessage.Txt NAV-DOP/NAV-PVT methods with callbacks ChangeStatusNAVDOP(bool) and ChangeStatusNAVPVT(bool) Renaming Example // 1.0.0 IAuthenticationService authentication = Vigram.Initial("YOUR_SDK_TOKEN"); IBluetoothService bluetooth = Vigram.BluetoothService; IPeripheralService peripheral = Vigram.PeripheralService(selectedPeripheral); INtripService ntrip = Vigram.NtripService; ILaserService laser = Vigram.LaserService(peripheral); Create these services once for the owning session and pass the instances around. Vigram.BluetoothService, Vigram.NtripService, Vigram.LaserService(...), and the other service factories do not return shared singletons. NMEA Handling peripheral.Nmea = message => { if (message is NmeaMessage.Gga gga) { ntripTask.SetGga(gga); } }; GPS Service Creation IGPSService now needs the Bluetooth service as well as the peripheral and NTRIP task. IGPSService gps = Vigram.GPSService( peripheral, bluetooth, ntripTask, automaticallyReconnectNtrip: true); NTRIP Error Handling Always wire both startup callbacks and the correction data error channel. ntripTask.Data((bytes, error) => { if (error != null) { Debug.LogError(error.Message); return; } Debug.Log(bytes.Length); }); ntripTask.Start( actionSuccess: () => Debug.Log("NTRIP connected"), actionFailure: error => Debug.LogError(error.Message)); GPS Disconnect Reasons IGPSService.ShouldDisconnect reports: ConfigurationFailed; DeviceNotFound; NtripDisrupted; InvalidGnssPosition. Use this callback for user-facing reconnect UI, diagnostics, and support logs.

articles/platforms-build.html.title: Platforms And Build | viDoc SDK for Unity
articles/platforms-build.html.keywords: Platforms And Build iOS The package includes native iOS plugins: Runtime/Plugins/iOS/BluetoothLE; Runtime/Plugins/iOS/MotionManager. The editor post-build processor Editor/PostBuildProcess.cs adds this key to the generated Info.plist: NSBluetoothAlwaysUsageDescription = Needed to work with viGram Check the final Info.plist after Xcode export, especially if the host application already provides its own Bluetooth permission text. Production applications should use user-facing permission text that matches the app experience. Android The Android plugin is located at: Runtime/Plugins/Android/BluetoothLE.androidlib The package AndroidManifest.xml declares: android.hardware.bluetooth_le; android.permission.BLUETOOTH up to API 30; android.permission.BLUETOOTH_ADMIN up to API 30; android.permission.BLUETOOTH_ADVERTISE; android.permission.BLUETOOTH_CONNECT; android.permission.BLUETOOTH_SCAN; android.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION; android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION; android.permission.WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE up to API 28. The androidlib build.gradle uses compileSdk 36, targetSdk 31, and minSdk 26. Android 12 and newer require runtime Bluetooth permissions for scan/connect operations. The host Unity application should request permissions before starting a scan and should handle denial with user-facing UI. Render Pipeline The SDK does not include rendering components, shaders, scenes, or visual prefabs. It is independent of Built-in Render Pipeline, URP, and HDRP. WebGL WebGL is not listed in package.json, README.md, or CHANGELOG.md as a supported target. The current package depends on mobile Bluetooth LE, Android/iOS native plugins, and a mobile motion bridge. Build Validation Checklist Install the package through Unity Package Manager. Select an iOS or Android build target. Verify platform permissions and generated platform metadata. Build and run on a physical device. Check IBluetoothService.State. Confirm that ObserveAvailableDevices receives viDoc devices. Connect to a selected BPeripheral. Subscribe to IPeripheralService.ConfigurationState before Start(). Confirm that Nmea receives GGA/GST messages. If NTRIP is used, verify mountpoint loading, task startup, and correction state. If laser features are used, confirm device capabilities through CurrentDevice.

articles/recipes.html.title: Practical Recipes | viDoc SDK for Unity
articles/recipes.html.keywords: Practical Recipes This section turns the public API into integration-oriented workflows. Each recipe focuses on a task that normally appears in a production Unity app. Own A Peripheral Session Use this pattern after the application has authenticated, scanned, and selected a BPeripheral. The complete first-run flow lives in Installation And First Run; this recipe focuses on keeping service ownership explicit. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class VidocPeripheralSession { private readonly IBluetoothService bluetooth; private readonly BPeripheral selectedPeripheral; private readonly IPeripheralService peripheral; public VidocPeripheralSession( IBluetoothService bluetooth, BPeripheral selectedPeripheral, ILogger logger = null) { this.bluetooth = bluetooth; this.selectedPeripheral = selectedPeripheral; peripheral = Vigram.PeripheralService(selectedPeripheral, logger: logger); } public IPeripheralService Peripheral => peripheral; public void Start() { bluetooth.StopScan(); bluetooth.Connect(selectedPeripheral); peripheral.State = state => Debug.Log($"Peripheral: {state}"); peripheral.ConfigurationState = state => Debug.Log($"Configuration: {state.GetType().Name}"); peripheral.Nmea = message => Debug.Log(message.Raw); peripheral.Start(); } public void Stop() { bluetooth.Disconnect(); } } The session receives the existing IBluetoothService; it does not call Vigram.BluetoothService again. Higher-level UI code can own this class and call Stop() when the session ends. Subscribe To NMEA Messages Use IPeripheralService.Nmea when the app needs raw NMEA sentences, GGA positions, GST accuracy, or TXT diagnostics. For best SDK behavior, configure the viDoc device to emit NMEA-0183 GGA and GST messages. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class NmeaConsumer { private NmeaMessage.Gga latestGga; public NmeaMessage.Gga LatestGga => latestGga; public void Attach(IPeripheralService peripheral) { peripheral.Nmea = message => { switch (message) { case NmeaMessage.Gga gga: latestGga = gga; Debug.Log($"GGA: {gga.Coordinate?.Latitude}, {gga.Coordinate?.Longitude}, quality: {gga.Quality}"); break; case NmeaMessage.Gst gst: Debug.Log($"GST accuracy: H={gst.Accuracy.Horizontal} m, V={gst.Accuracy.Vertical} m"); break; case NmeaMessage.Txt txt: Debug.Log($"TXT: {txt.Message}"); break; } }; } } Keep the latest NmeaMessage.Gga if the app will create an NTRIP task. NTRIP needs a current position so the caster can return relevant corrections. Read Device Version, Battery, And Capabilities Use this recipe after IPeripheralService.Start() to populate diagnostics UI and to decide whether laser features should be shown. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class DeviceInfoReader { public void Attach(IPeripheralService peripheral) { peripheral.CurrentDevice = device => { Debug.Log($"Device type: {device.TypeOfDevice}"); Debug.Log($"Front laser: {device.HasFrontLaser}"); Debug.Log($"Bottom laser: {device.HasBottomLaser}"); Debug.Log($"Housing: {device.GetHousing}"); Debug.Log($"Mount: {device.GetMount}"); if (device.TypeOfDevice == DeviceVersion.ViDocLight) { Debug.Log("Disable laser controls for viDoc Light."); } if (device.TypeOfDevice == DeviceVersion.ViDocWithOldSoftware) { Debug.LogWarning("Device software is old. Ask the user to update firmware."); } if (device.TypeOfDevice == DeviceVersion.Unknown) { Debug.LogError("Unsupported viDoc device."); } }; peripheral.RequestBattery(battery => Debug.Log($"Battery: {battery.Percentage}%")); peripheral.RequestVersion(version => Debug.Log($"Hardware: {version.Hardware}, software: {version.Software}")); } } The exact UI decision is application-specific. A common pattern is to hide laser buttons for ViDocLight, show a firmware update warning for old software, and collect logs for unknown devices. Handle Device And Satellite Messages Use DeviceMessages when you want one callback for all device-level responses, including measurements, battery, version, IMU, and laser state. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class MessageRouter { public void Attach(IPeripheralService peripheral) { peripheral.DeviceMessages = message => { switch (message) { case DeviceMessage.Battery battery: Debug.Log($"Battery: {battery.Percentage}%"); break; case DeviceMessage.Version version: Debug.Log($"Version: {version.Software}"); break; case DeviceMessage.Measurement measurement: Debug.Log($"Distance: {measurement.Distance} mm, quality: {measurement.Quality}"); break; case DeviceMessage.LaserState laserState: Debug.Log($"Laser state: {laserState.State}"); break; } }; peripheral.SatelliteMessages = message => { switch (message) { case SatelliteMessage.Dop dop: Debug.Log($"HDOP: {dop.HorizontalDop}, VDOP: {dop.VerticalDop}"); break; case SatelliteMessage.Pvt pvt: Debug.Log($"Satellites used: {pvt.SatelliteCount}"); break; case SatelliteMessage.Acknowledge acknowledge: Debug.Log($"GNSS request acknowledged: {acknowledge.Result}"); break; } }; } } Use typed request callbacks when the app asks for one specific value. Use broad message callbacks when the app needs to monitor the whole session. Configure GNSS Constellations Use these methods to control which navigation systems are enabled. GPS is treated as the base system and should not be disabled through ChangeStatusGNSS. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class GnssConstellationConfigurator { public void Configure(IPeripheralService peripheral) { peripheral.ActivateAllConstellationGNSS(); peripheral.GetCurrentStatusGNSS( NavigationSystemValue.NavigationSystemType.GALILEO, status => Debug.Log($"GALILEO enabled: {status.IsEnabled}")); peripheral.ChangeStatusGNSS( NavigationSystemValue.NavigationSystemType.GLONASS, activate: true); peripheral.ChangeStatusGNSS( NavigationSystemValue.NavigationSystemType.BEIDOU, activate: true); } } Use this in a settings or diagnostics screen. Avoid changing GNSS configuration repeatedly during active measurement unless the product explicitly needs it. Configure NAV-DOP, NAV-PVT, Elevation, Rate, And Dynamic State The SDK exposes typed helpers for common viDoc GNSS configuration. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class GnssMessageConfigurator { public void Configure(IPeripheralService peripheral) { peripheral.ChangeStatusNAVDOP(activate: true); peripheral.ChangeStatusNAVPVT(activate: true); peripheral.SetMinimumElevation(ElevationValue.Elevation.Angle10); peripheral.GetCurrentMinimumElevation(elevation => Debug.Log($"Elevation mask: {elevation.Value}")); peripheral.SetChangingRateOfMessages(RateValue.Rate.Hertz7); peripheral.GetChangingRateOfMessages(rate => Debug.Log($"Message rate: {rate.Value}")); peripheral.SetDynamicState(DynamicStateType.Pedestrian); peripheral.GetCurrentDynamicState(state => Debug.Log($"Dynamic state: {state.Value}")); } } Recommended defaults depend on the app domain. Walking capture workflows usually use DynamicStateType.Pedestrian; fixed installations can use DynamicStateType.Stationary. Fetch NTRIP Mountpoints Use INtripService.Mountpoints to present available caster mountpoints to the user or to select one automatically. using System.Collections.Generic; using System.Linq; using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class NtripMountpointLoader { private readonly INtripService ntrip; public NtripMountpointLoader(INtripService ntrip) { this.ntrip = ntrip; } public void Load() { NtripConnectionInformation connection = new( hostname: "caster.example.com", port: 2101, username: "user", password: "password", forceHttpsConnection: true, forceHttpsMountpointsConnection: true); ntrip.Mountpoints( connection, actionSuccess: mountpoints => OnMountpoints(connection, mountpoints), actionFailure: error => Debug.LogError($"Mountpoint request failed: {error.Message}")); } private void OnMountpoints( NtripConnectionInformation connection, List<NtripMountPoint> mountpoints) { IEnumerable<NtripMountPoint> rtcmMountpoints = mountpoints .Where(point => point.Format.IsRTCM && point.RequiresNMEA); foreach (NtripMountPoint point in rtcmMountpoints) { Debug.Log($"{point.Name} {point.CountryCode} {point.NetworkName}"); } } } Create INtripService once for the NTRIP flow and pass it into helpers like this loader. The viDoc correction flow expects mountpoints that accept NMEA input. Filtering by RequiresNMEA and RTCM-like data formats is a useful first pass, but the final selection still depends on provider rules and field requirements. INtripService ntrip = Vigram.NtripService; NtripMountpointLoader loader = new NtripMountpointLoader(ntrip); NtripRunner runner = new NtripRunner(ntrip); Start An NTRIP Task Create the NTRIP task after you have a selected mountpoint and a recent NmeaMessage.Gga. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class NtripRunner { private readonly INtripService ntrip; public NtripRunner(INtripService ntrip) { this.ntrip = ntrip; } public INtripTaskService CreateAndStart( NtripConnectionInformation connection, string mountPoint, NmeaMessage.Gga latestGga) { INtripTaskService task = ntrip.Task(connection, mountPoint, latestGga); task.NtripState(state => Debug.Log(state.Description())); task.Data((bytes, error) => { if (error != null) { Debug.LogError($"Correction stream failed: {error.Message}"); return; } Debug.Log($"Correction bytes: {bytes.Length}"); }); task.Start( actionSuccess: () => Debug.Log("NTRIP connected"), actionFailure: error => Debug.LogError($"NTRIP connection failed: {error.Message}")); return task; } public void UpdatePosition(INtripTaskService task, NmeaMessage.Gga latestGga) { task.SetGga(latestGga); } } Keep the returned INtripTaskService for the active correction stream. Call Disconnect() on that same instance when the session ends. If the app moves significantly, keep the NTRIP task updated with fresh GGA messages. Start GPS With Automatic NTRIP Reconnect Use IGPSService when you want one service to combine corrected location data, NTRIP state, correction status, and disconnect signals. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class GpsRunner { private IGPSService gps; public void StartGps( IPeripheralService peripheral, IBluetoothService bluetooth, INtripTaskService ntripTask) { gps = Vigram.GPSService( peripheral, bluetooth, ntripTask, automaticallyReconnectNtrip: true); gps.Coordinate(coordinate => { if (coordinate.HasValue) { Debug.Log($"{coordinate.Value.Latitude}, {coordinate.Value.Longitude}"); } }); gps.TimestampedCoordinate((timestamp, coordinate) => Debug.Log($"{timestamp:o}: {coordinate.Latitude}, {coordinate.Longitude}")); gps.HorizontalAccuracy(value => Debug.Log($"Horizontal accuracy: {value} m")); gps.VerticalAccuracy(value => Debug.Log($"Vertical accuracy: {value} m")); gps.Quality(quality => Debug.Log($"GPS quality: {quality}")); gps.Hdop(hdop => Debug.Log($"HDOP: {hdop}")); gps.IsCorrecting(isCorrecting => Debug.Log($"Correcting: {isCorrecting}")); gps.ShouldDisconnect(signal => Debug.LogWarning($"GPS disconnect reason: {signal.DisconnectReason}")); gps.Start(state => Debug.Log(state.Description())); } } Keep the IGPSService instance for the active GPS session. Use ShouldDisconnect to switch the UI into a recoverable state. For example, show a reconnect button for NtripDisrupted, ask the user to move into better GNSS conditions for InvalidGnssPosition, or restart discovery for DeviceNotFound. Record A Laser Measurement Use ILaserService.Record for a measurement session. Use device capabilities to hide laser UI on viDoc Light. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class LaserMeasurementRunner { private readonly ILaserService laser; public LaserMeasurementRunner(IPeripheralService peripheral) { laser = Vigram.LaserService(peripheral); } public void Measure() { laser.GetLasersStatus(state => Debug.Log($"Laser status: {state.State}")); LaserConfiguration configuration = new( shotMode: LaserConfiguration.ShotMode.Auto, position: LaserConfiguration.Position.Bottom, duration: 10); laser.Record( configuration, actionSuccess: measurement => { Debug.Log($"Distance: {measurement.Distance} mm"); Debug.Log($"Quality: {measurement.Quality} ({measurement.QualityRaw})"); }, actionFailure: error => Debug.LogError($"Laser measurement failed: {error.Message}")); } } Create one ILaserService for the peripheral session and reuse it for status, on/off commands, and recording. For protocol version 1.0, laser duration is clamped to the supported range of 5 to 60 seconds. Turn A Laser On Or Off Use explicit laser state control only when your UI needs it. Measurement sessions can often rely on Record. // Reuse the ILaserService that belongs to this peripheral session. laser.TurnLaserOn(LaserConfiguration.Position.Bottom); laser.TurnLaserOff(LaserConfiguration.Position.Bottom); Front laser controls should be shown only when CurrentDevice reports HasFrontLaser. Request IMU Data Use IMU requests for diagnostics or orientation-related workflows. using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class ImuReader { public void Read(IPeripheralService peripheral) { peripheral.RequestIMUAngle(angle => Debug.Log($"Nick: {angle.NickAngle}, roll: {angle.RollAngle}")); peripheral.RequestIMURotation(rotation => Debug.Log($"Nick rate: {rotation.NickRate}, roll rate: {rotation.RollRate}, yaw rate: {rotation.YawRate}")); peripheral.RequestIMUCalibrationStatus(status => Debug.Log($"IMU calibration count: {status.Times}")); } } Only expose calibration actions in UI if the product has a clear calibration workflow for the user. Log A Full Session Use a logger when debugging field issues, validating NTRIP behavior, or collecting support data. Create the logger once before creating the logged peripheral service. using System.IO; using UnityEngine; using ViGram; public class LoggedSessionFactory { public IPeripheralService Create(BPeripheral selectedPeripheral) { string file = Path.Combine(Application.persistentDataPath, "vidoc-session.csv"); ILogger logger = Vigram.LoggerService(file); return Vigram.PeripheralService(selectedPeripheral, logger: logger); } } Attach the logger before Start() to capture startup and configuration messages. Treat logs as support data: define retention, upload, and deletion behavior in the host application.

articles/services.html.title: Core Services | viDoc SDK for Unity
articles/services.html.keywords: Core Services Vigram Vigram is the public static factory surface. Most members below create new service objects; they are not singleton accessors. Store returned services in fields owned by your session, screen, or coordinator and pass those same instances to dependent components. Factory Returns Purpose Initial(string token) IAuthenticationService Validate SDK access TokenIsValid AuthenticationResult Read the cached authentication result BluetoothService IBluetoothService Bluetooth LE scan, connect, and disconnect PeripheralService(...) IPeripheralService viDoc protocol, messages, requests, and configuration NtripService INtripService Mountpoint discovery and NTRIP task creation GPSService(...) IGPSService GPS aggregation, NTRIP state, correction state, reconnect signals LaserService(IPeripheralService) ILaserService Laser state and distance measurements DeviceService() IDeviceService Unity device motion updates LoggerService(string filename) ILogger File logging for SDK events Recommended lifetime model: Service Typical owner IAuthenticationService Startup/authentication flow IBluetoothService One active viDoc session or device picker screen IPeripheralService One selected BPeripheral session INtripService Session object or NTRIP configuration screen INtripTaskService One selected NTRIP mountpoint/correction stream IGPSService One corrected GPS session ILaserService One peripheral session that exposes laser UI IDeviceService One motion-observation owner ILogger One logged session/file IAuthenticationService Use Vigram.Initial(token).Check(...) before Bluetooth, peripheral, NTRIP, GPS, or laser work. Vigram.Initial("YOUR_SDK_TOKEN").Check(result => { if (result is AuthenticationResult.Success) { Debug.Log("Authorized"); } if (result is AuthenticationResult.Error error) { Debug.LogError($"Auth failed: {error.Enum} {error.Message}"); } }); Failure values are represented by AuthenticationError. IBluetoothService Owns Bluetooth state, scanning, available BPeripheral updates, connection, and disconnection. Member Use State(...) Observe platform Bluetooth availability ObserveAvailableDevices(...) Receive the current viDoc candidate list StartScan() Start scanning StopScan() Stop scanning and clear available devices Connect(BPeripheral) Connect to the selected device Disconnect() Disconnect the active device Create one IBluetoothService for a viDoc session and reuse it for scanning, connecting, GPS service creation, and cleanup. Calling Vigram.BluetoothService again creates another service instance. BPeripheral BPeripheral identifies a discovered viDoc device. Member Meaning Name Display name received from Bluetooth discovery SerialNumber Serial-like value derived from the device name IPeripheralService Main viDoc protocol service. It receives parsed NMEA, device, and satellite messages and sends high-level requests to the connected device. Important callbacks: Callback Use Nmea Receive NmeaMessage.Gga, NmeaMessage.Gst, and NmeaMessage.Txt CurrentDevice Receive parsed viDoc model/capability information DeviceMessages Receive battery, version, measurement, IMU, laser state, and other device messages SatelliteMessages Receive GNSS configuration and satellite responses State Observe connected/disconnected peripheral state ConfigurationState Observe startup configuration progress or failure ViDocState Receive viDoc warning/error/notice/user state messages ViDocResetState Observe reset progress PpkMeasurementsState Observe PPK measurement state Debug Receive SDK diagnostic messages during integration Important methods: Method Use Start() Initialize the connected peripheral session RequestVersion(...) Request hardware/software version RequestBattery(...) Request battery percentage RequestIMUAngle(...) and related IMU methods Request IMU readings GetCurrentStatusGNSS(...) Read enabled state for a navigation system ChangeStatusGNSS(...) Enable or disable a navigation system except GPS ActivateAllConstellationGNSS() Enable all supported constellations ChangeStatusNAVDOP(...) Enable or disable NAV-DOP messages ChangeStatusNAVPVT(...) Enable or disable NAV-PVT messages GetCurrentMinimumElevation(...) Read the current elevation mask SetMinimumElevation(...) Set the elevation mask GetChangingRateOfMessages(...) Read GNSS message rate SetChangingRateOfMessages(...) Set GNSS message rate SetDynamicState(...) Set pedestrian or stationary dynamic model GetCurrentDynamicState(...) Read the active dynamic model ResetViDoc() Request viDoc reset Send(byte[] data, int chunkSize) is public, but it is a low-level escape hatch. Prefer the typed methods above for application code. INtripService And INtripTaskService INtripService fetches mountpoints and creates INtripTaskService instances. Keep one INtripService for the NTRIP flow and keep the returned task for one correction stream. Type Responsibility INtripService.Mountpoints(...) Load available mountpoints from a caster INtripService.Task(...) Create a correction task for a mountpoint and current GGA INtripTaskService.Start(...) Start the correction connection INtripTaskService.Data(...) Receive correction bytes or terminal stream errors INtripTaskService.SetGga(...) Update the task with the current position INtripTaskService.Disconnect() Stop the correction stream See Fetch NTRIP Mountpoints and Start An NTRIP Task. IGPSService Combines IPeripheralService, IBluetoothService, and INtripTaskService. Callback/method Use Coordinate(...) Current coordinate TimestampedCoordinate(...) Current coordinate with timestamp HorizontalAccuracy(...) / VerticalAccuracy(...) Accuracy values in meters Quality(...) GPS quality indicator Hdop(...) Horizontal dilution of precision IsCorrecting(...) Whether corrected data is active ShouldDisconnect(...) Structured disconnect/reconnect signal Start(...) Start NTRIP/GPS processing Reconnect() Attempt reconnect See Start GPS With Automatic NTRIP Reconnect. ILaserService Created on top of an active IPeripheralService. Method Use GetLasersStatus(...) Read current laser state TurnLaserOn(...) Turn on the selected laser position TurnLaserOff(...) Turn off the selected laser position Record(...) Start a measurement session Keep one laser service for the peripheral session. For devices without lasers, hide or disable laser features based on CurrentDevice. See Record A Laser Measurement. IDeviceService Exposes mobile device motion updates and is independent from the viDoc peripheral session. IDeviceService device = Vigram.DeviceService(); device.StartDeviceMotionUpdates(motion => Debug.Log(motion.Attitude)); device.StopDeviceMotionUpdates(); Keep the same IDeviceService instance so StopDeviceMotionUpdates() stops the updates that were started by that owner. ILogger Vigram.LoggerService(filename) creates an SDK logger. Attach it before IPeripheralService.Start() so startup, configuration, NMEA, device, and satellite events are captured from the beginning of the session. If the path is relative, the SDK writes under Application.persistentDataPath. Android also supports the android-documents:// scheme for the Documents directory. See Log A Full Session.

articles/troubleshooting.html.title: Troubleshooting | viDoc SDK for Unity
articles/troubleshooting.html.keywords: Troubleshooting Authentication Fails Check the AuthenticationResult.Error value and message. Vigram.Initial("YOUR_SDK_TOKEN").Check(result => { if (result is AuthenticationResult.Error error) { Debug.LogError($"{error.Enum}: {error.Message}"); } }); Common causes: the token is missing; the token does not match the application bundle/package; the SDK was deactivated; offline authorization is missing or expired; the device has no connectivity when a fresh online check is required. Bluetooth Scan Finds No Devices Check: IBluetoothService.State; Android runtime Bluetooth and location permissions; the generated iOS NSBluetoothAlwaysUsageDescription; that the app is running on a physical iOS or Android device; that the viDoc device is powered on and close enough; that scanning is not stopped immediately by app lifecycle code; that the UI is using the latest list from ObserveAvailableDevices. bluetooth.State(state => Debug.Log($"Bluetooth state: {state}")); bluetooth.ObserveAvailableDevices(devices => Debug.Log($"Device count: {devices.Count}")); bluetooth.StartScan(); Peripheral Does Not Configure Subscribe to IPeripheralService.ConfigurationState, State, and Debug before calling Start(). peripheral.State = state => Debug.Log($"Peripheral: {state}"); peripheral.ConfigurationState = state => Debug.Log($"Configuration: {state.GetType().Name}"); peripheral.Debug = message => Debug.Log(message); peripheral.Start(); Typical causes include Bluetooth disconnects, unsupported device version, old firmware, denied access, write timeouts, or configuration values that are not supported by the connected device. No NMEA GGA Or GST Messages Arrive Check: IPeripheralService.Start() was called after Bluetooth connect; Nmea was assigned before or immediately after startup; viDoc is configured to emit NMEA-0183 GGA and GST; the device has enough GNSS visibility; logs contain incoming NMEA or satellite messages. peripheral.Nmea = message => Debug.Log(message.Raw); Without a recent NmeaMessage.Gga, the application cannot create a useful NTRIP task. NTRIP Mountpoint Loading Fails Use both success and failure callbacks. INtripService ntrip = Vigram.NtripService; ntrip.Mountpoints( connection, actionSuccess: points => Debug.Log(points.Count), actionFailure: error => Debug.LogError(error.Message)); Check host, port, username, password, HTTPS flags, network availability, and provider access rules. Some providers require HTTPS for mountpoint discovery or correction streams. NTRIP Does Not Connect Wire NtripState, Start(actionSuccess, actionFailure), and Data. ntripTask.NtripState(state => Debug.Log(state.Description())); ntripTask.Data((bytes, error) => { if (error != null) { Debug.LogError(error.Message); } }); ntripTask.Start( actionSuccess: () => Debug.Log("Connected"), actionFailure: error => Debug.LogError(error.Message)); Check: the selected mountpoint name; credentials; whether the mountpoint requires NMEA input; whether the initial NmeaMessage.Gga is recent; whether the server returns UNAUTHORIZED, MOUNTPOINT_NOT_FOUND, TIMEOUT, or BAD_RESPONSE. Correction Stream Stops In 1.0.0, correction stream errors arrive as the second argument of Data. ntripTask.Data((bytes, error) => { if (error != null) { Debug.LogError(error.Message); return; } Debug.Log($"Correction bytes: {bytes.Length}"); }); If IGPSService is created with automaticallyReconnectNtrip: true, the SDK attempts reconnect after at least one successful NTRIP connection. Use ShouldDisconnect to update UI and logs. GPS Reports A Disconnect Signal IGPSService.ShouldDisconnect can report: ConfigurationFailed; DeviceNotFound; NtripDisrupted; InvalidGnssPosition. Use the reason to choose a recovery path. gps.ShouldDisconnect(signal => { switch (signal.DisconnectReason) { case GPSDisconnectSignal.Reason.NtripDisrupted: Debug.LogWarning("Show NTRIP reconnect UI."); break; case GPSDisconnectSignal.Reason.InvalidGnssPosition: Debug.LogWarning("Ask the user to move to better GNSS visibility."); break; default: Debug.LogWarning(signal.DisconnectReason); break; } }); Laser Measurement Fails Check: the connected device is not DeviceVersion.ViDocLight; CurrentDevice reports the requested laser position as available; the selected LaserConfiguration.Position is valid for the device; duration is finite and within the supported range; actionFailure is logged; the device is not busy with another operation. laser.Record( configuration, actionSuccess: measurement => Debug.Log(measurement.Distance), actionFailure: error => Debug.LogError(error.Message)); For protocol version 1.0, laser duration is clamped to 5-60 seconds. Logs Pass an ILogger into PeripheralService before startup. See Log A Full Session for the setup pattern. If a path is relative, the SDK writes under Application.persistentDataPath. On Android, use the android-documents:// scheme when the app needs to write into the Documents directory. Logs are especially useful for support cases involving authentication, scan/connect, peripheral configuration, NMEA availability, NTRIP state, and laser measurement failures.

index.html.keywords: viDoc SDK for Unity com.vigram.sdk-gh1 connects Unity mobile applications to viDoc devices over Bluetooth LE and exposes public APIs for GNSS/NMEA data, NTRIP corrections, laser measurements, device state, logging, and mobile motion data. Key Facts Parameter Value SDK version 1.0.0 Unity package com.vigram.sdk-gh1 Minimum Unity version 2021.3 Target platforms iOS, Android Package path Packages/com.vigram.sdk-gh1 Render pipeline Independent of Built-in, URP, and HDRP Audience This documentation is written for Unity developers integrating a viDoc device into a mobile application, QA engineers validating Bluetooth/GNSS/NTRIP behavior, and SDK maintainers who need a clear map of the public API surface. Start Here Install the package and run the first scan in Installation And First Run. Check service ownership rules in Core Services. Use Practical Recipes for NMEA, NTRIP, GPS, GNSS configuration, laser, IMU, and logging workflows. Review Platforms And Build before mobile builds. Use Troubleshooting when a field session fails. What This Site Covers Unity Package Manager installation. The runtime flow from SDK token validation to Bluetooth scan, device selection, connection, and peripheral startup.
